content.problemStatement:
You are given an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] consisting of NNN positive integers.

You are also given a constant KKK, using which you can perform the following operation on AAA:


Choose two distinct indices iii and jjj such that Ai+Aj≤KA_i + A_j \le KAi​+Aj​≤K, and remove either AiA_iAi​ or AjA_jAj​ from AAA.
Is it possible to obtain an array consisting of only one element using several (possibly, zero) such operations?


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print `"YES"` if it is possible to obtain an array consisting of only one element using the given operation, otherwise print `"NO"`.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line has two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to reduce the array to exactly one element using the allowed operations, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test 1: array already has one element. Test 2: min+max = 2+2 = 4 > 3, no pair satisfies the condition, so impossible. Test 3: min=1, max=5, 1+5=6 ≤ 7, therefore the smallest element can be paired with every other element and we can delete them one by one.
First case: min+max = 2+3 = 5 ≤ 5, so YES. Second case: min=1, max=5, 1+5=6 ≤ 10, so YES.
min+max = 4+7 = 11 > 10, the largest element cannot be paired with any other, thus reduction to one element is impossible.

solution.approach: The operation can delete an element only if it can be paired with another element whose sum does not exceed K. Let m be the minimum element of the current array and M be the maximum element. If m + M <= K, then m can be paired with every other element (because any other element x satisfies m + x <= m + M <= K). We can repeatedly delete the other element while keeping m, finally leaving a single element. Conversely, if m + M > K, the maximum element M cannot be paired with any element (even the smallest one), so M can never be removed and also cannot be used to remove others, making it impossible to end with one element unless N==1. Therefore the answer is YES iff N == 1 or (minimum + maximum <= K). The algorithm for each test case scans the array once to obtain min and max and checks the condition.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (single pass to find min and max). Overall O(sum of N) ≤ O(2·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.
